title: Preparing for Your Operation
excerpt: A complete guide to preparing yourself before surgery including fasting instructions and what to bring to hospital.
content:
Preparing well for your operation helps ensure it goes smoothly and reduces your risk of complications. Your surgical team will give you specific instructions but this guide covers the general principles that apply to most planned procedures.

Fasting Instructions

Most operations requiring a general anaesthetic require you to fast beforehand. You should have nothing to eat for at least six hours before your operation. You can usually drink clear fluids (water, black tea or coffee without milk) up to two hours before. Follow your specific pre-admission letter exactly - instructions vary by anaesthetic type.

Medications

Tell your surgical team about all medications you take including over-the-counter medicines, supplements and herbal remedies. Some medications must be stopped before surgery (certain blood thinners, diabetic medications, NSAIDs). Do not stop any prescribed medication without specific instructions from your surgical team.

What to Bring

Bring your pre-admission paperwork and any letters from your consultant. Bring a list of all your current medications with dosages. Bring comfortable loose clothing for after the operation. Leave valuables at home. Bring your reading glasses if you use them since you will need to sign consent forms.

  • Remove nail varnish from fingers and toes before admission
  • Do not apply creams, lotions or perfume on the day
  • Arrange for someone to collect you - you cannot drive after a general anaesthetic
  • Arrange for an adult to stay with you for the first 24 hours at home
author: Clinical Information Team

title: What to Expect on the Day of Your Operation
excerpt: A walkthrough of what happens from admission through to surgery including what each stage involves.
content:
Knowing what to expect on the day of your operation reduces anxiety and helps you feel in control. Here is what typically happens from the moment you arrive at the hospital.

Admission and Pre-Assessment

You will be admitted to a ward or day surgery unit. A nurse will check your observations (blood pressure, heart rate, temperature, oxygen levels), confirm your medication history and check you have fasted as instructed. Your anaesthetist and surgeon will visit to confirm the procedure, answer your questions and ask you to sign consent forms.

The Anaesthetic

If you are having a general anaesthetic, you will be taken to the anaesthetic room adjoining the operating theatre. The anaesthetist will insert a cannula (thin plastic tube) into your vein and administer the anaesthetic drugs. You will lose consciousness within seconds and will not be aware of anything during the operation.

In the Recovery Room

When the operation is complete you will be taken to the recovery room where specialist nurses monitor your vital signs as the anaesthetic wears off. You may feel groggy, cold, nauseous or experience some pain. The team will manage any discomfort before returning you to the ward.

  • Ask any questions you have before signing consent
  • Tell your team about any allergies or previous reactions to anaesthetic
  • You will be kept nil-by-mouth until you are fully awake after a general anaesthetic
  • Most people can eat and drink within a few hours of a straightforward procedure
author: Clinical Information Team

title: Your Pre-Assessment Clinic Appointment
excerpt: What happens at a pre-assessment clinic and why it is an important part of your surgical preparation.
content:
A pre-assessment clinic appointment takes place before your planned operation. It is an opportunity for the team to check your health, review your medications and identify anything that might affect your surgery or anaesthetic.

What Happens at Pre-Assessment

A specialist nurse will take a detailed medical history, ask about previous anaesthetics and surgeries, review your current medications and carry out basic health checks (ECG, blood tests, blood pressure, lung function). They will explain what to expect before, during and after your operation and answer your questions.

Why Pre-Assessment Matters

Pre-assessment allows the team to identify and address any health issues that could increase your surgical risk. Optimising blood pressure, blood glucose, stopping smoking or adjusting medications before surgery significantly improves outcomes. Identifying issues at this stage prevents cancellations on the day.

  • Bring a complete list of all medications including supplements
  • Tell the team about any recent illness or changes in your health
  • Ask about what to do with each of your regular medications before surgery
  • This is the best time to ask questions about the procedure and recovery
author: Clinical Information Team

title: Recovering at Home After Surgery
excerpt: Essential advice for a safe and comfortable recovery at home following your operation.
content:
Going home after surgery is an important milestone, but the recovery period requires careful attention to wound care, activity levels and watching for warning signs. This guide helps you recover safely and comfortably.

Wound Care

Keep your wound clean and dry as instructed by your surgical team. Most surgical wounds are closed with dissolvable stitches, surgical staples or adhesive strips. Your discharge letter will specify when and how to clean the wound, when you can get it wet and whether you need a wound check appointment.

Managing Pain at Home

Some discomfort is normal after surgery. Take pain relief as prescribed and at regular intervals rather than waiting until pain is severe. A combination of paracetamol and ibuprofen (if suitable for you) manages post-operative pain effectively for most people. Do not stop prescribed medications without advice.

Returning to Activity

Follow your specific discharge instructions about when you can return to different activities. General guidance: light activity as soon as you feel able; driving only when you can perform an emergency stop safely; return to work depends on the nature of your job and the operation. Avoid heavy lifting until your surgeon advises it is safe.

  • Seek urgent medical advice if you develop fever above 38 degrees, increasing pain, swelling or discharge from your wound
  • Attend all follow-up appointments
  • Rest when you need to - recovery is not linear
  • Eat well and stay hydrated to support healing
author: Clinical Information Team

title: Warning Signs After Surgery
excerpt: Which symptoms after surgery require urgent medical attention and what to do.
content:
Most people recover from surgery without complications. However, it is important to know which symptoms should prompt you to seek urgent medical advice rather than waiting for a scheduled follow-up appointment.

Signs of Wound Infection

Increasing redness, warmth or swelling around the wound, discharge (especially if thick, yellow or green), increasing rather than improving pain, and fever (temperature above 38 degrees Celsius) are all signs of possible wound infection. Contact your ward, GP or call 111 promptly. Do not wait.

Signs of Blood Clot (DVT or PE)

After surgery, particularly lower limb or pelvic surgery, there is a risk of deep vein thrombosis (DVT) and pulmonary embolism (PE). Signs of DVT include calf pain, swelling and redness in one leg. Signs of PE include sudden breathlessness, chest pain, coughing up blood or a fast heart rate. These are medical emergencies - call 999 immediately.

Other Urgent Symptoms

Sudden severe abdominal pain, difficulty breathing, chest pain, collapse or unconsciousness, inability to pass urine for more than 12 hours, or any symptom that seriously concerns you should prompt immediate contact with emergency services or your surgical ward directly.

  • Keep your ward telephone number accessible after discharge
  • Call 999 for chest pain or difficulty breathing
  • Call 111 if unsure whether your symptoms need urgent attention
  • Do not wait for routine appointments if you have a new concerning symptom
author: Clinical Information Team

title: Understanding Day Surgery
excerpt: What day surgery means what to expect and how to prepare for a same-day procedure.
content:
Day surgery means you are admitted and discharged on the same day as your operation. It is now the most common type of surgical admission in the UK. Modern anaesthetic and surgical techniques mean many procedures that previously required overnight stays can safely be done as day cases.

Are You Suitable for Day Surgery?

Most people are suitable for day surgery if they have a responsible adult to take them home and stay with them for 24 hours, and if their home environment is suitable for recovery. Some medical conditions (poorly controlled diabetes, severe heart or lung disease) may mean an overnight stay is safer. Your pre-assessment team will advise.

On the Day

Arrive at your designated time and expect to be at the hospital for several hours including preparation, surgery and recovery time. Recovery from day surgery anaesthetics is carefully managed to ensure you are fully awake, comfortable and safe before discharge. Your nurse will go through discharge instructions with you and your carer before you leave.

  • You must not drive, operate machinery or make legal decisions for 24 hours after a general anaesthetic
  • Do not travel alone - your escort must be present at discharge
  • Have pain relief available at home before your procedure
  • Follow all wound care and activity instructions given at discharge
author: Clinical Information Team

title: Understanding Your Heart Condition
excerpt: An introduction to the most common heart conditions and what they mean for patients.
content:
Receiving a diagnosis of a heart condition can be frightening, but most heart conditions are very manageable with the right treatment and lifestyle changes. Understanding your diagnosis is the first step towards living well with it.

Coronary Heart Disease

Coronary heart disease (CHD) occurs when the arteries supplying the heart muscle become narrowed by a build-up of fatty deposits (atherosclerosis). This can cause angina (chest pain on exertion) and heart attacks. Treatment includes medications, lifestyle changes and in some cases coronary angioplasty or bypass surgery.

Heart Failure

Heart failure does not mean the heart has stopped. It means the heart is not pumping as effectively as it should be. Symptoms include breathlessness, fatigue and ankle swelling. It can be caused by previous heart attacks, high blood pressure or valve disease. Medication, lifestyle changes and cardiac rehabilitation significantly improve both symptoms and life expectancy.

Atrial Fibrillation

Atrial fibrillation (AF) is an irregular, often rapid heart rhythm. It increases the risk of stroke significantly. Treatment includes medications to control heart rate and rhythm, and anticoagulants to reduce stroke risk. Many people with AF live normal, full lives with appropriate treatment.

  • Attend all follow-up appointments with your cardiologist
  • Know your medications and why you take them
  • Cardiac rehabilitation is highly effective if offered - attend every session
  • Lifestyle changes (not smoking, exercise, diet) improve outcomes for all heart conditions
author: Clinical Information Team

title: Living with Heart Failure
excerpt: Practical guidance for patients living with heart failure including monitoring symptoms and self-management.
content:
Living well with heart failure is possible with the right self-management strategies. The goal is to reduce symptoms, prevent deterioration and avoid hospital admissions. This guide covers the key strategies.

Daily Weight Monitoring

Weigh yourself every morning after using the toilet and before eating, wearing similar clothing. Record the weight. A gain of more than 2 kg in two days or 3 kg in a week often indicates fluid retention and should prompt you to contact your heart failure nurse or GP before it becomes serious enough to require hospitalisation.

Fluid and Salt Restriction

Your cardiologist may advise limiting fluid intake to 1.5 to 2 litres per day. Reducing salt intake is important for almost all heart failure patients. Excess salt causes fluid retention which makes the heart work harder and worsens breathlessness. Read labels and choose low-sodium options.

  • Take your medications exactly as prescribed - never stop without advice
  • Know your target weight and contact your team if you are outside it
  • Attend your heart failure clinic appointments regularly
  • Gentle, regular exercise approved by your team improves symptoms and quality of life
author: Clinical Information Team

title: Understanding Cardiac Tests and Procedures
excerpt: What to expect from common cardiac investigations including ECG angiogram and echocardiogram.
content:
If you have been referred for cardiac tests or procedures, understanding what is involved helps reduce anxiety and ensures you prepare correctly. Here is an overview of the most common investigations.

ECG (Electrocardiogram)

An ECG records the electrical activity of your heart. Sticky electrodes are placed on your chest, arms and legs and connected to a machine. The test is entirely painless and takes around 10 minutes. It detects abnormal heart rhythms, signs of a previous heart attack and other cardiac abnormalities.

Echocardiogram

An echocardiogram is an ultrasound scan of the heart. A probe is placed on your chest and uses sound waves to create an image of the heart beating. It shows the heart's structure and how well it is pumping. The test is painless and takes around 45 minutes. No preparation is usually required.

Coronary Angiogram

A coronary angiogram examines the blood vessels supplying the heart. A thin tube (catheter) is inserted into a blood vessel in your wrist or groin and guided to the coronary arteries. Contrast dye is injected and X-ray images are taken. If narrowings are found, angioplasty (stenting) may be performed at the same time. The procedure takes 30 to 60 minutes under local anaesthetic.

  • Follow all preparation instructions specific to each test
  • Ask your cardiologist what the test results will mean for your management
  • Bring a list of all your medications to every investigation appointment
  • Most cardiac tests are performed as outpatients with no overnight stay required
author: Clinical Information Team

title: Cardiac Rehabilitation
excerpt: What cardiac rehabilitation is why it matters and what to expect from a programme.
content:
Cardiac rehabilitation (cardiac rehab) is a structured programme of exercise, education and psychological support offered to people who have had a heart attack, heart surgery or been diagnosed with heart failure. Research consistently shows it reduces the risk of further heart attacks and improves quality of life.

What a Programme Includes

A typical cardiac rehab programme runs for six to eight weeks and includes supervised exercise sessions (usually twice per week), education sessions on heart disease, medications, diet and risk factor management, and psychological support to manage anxiety and depression - which are very common after a heart event.

The Benefits

Attending cardiac rehab reduces the risk of a further heart attack by up to 20-25%. It improves exercise tolerance, reduces symptoms, improves psychological wellbeing and helps people return to work and normal activities sooner. Yet less than half of eligible patients attend. If you are offered it, go.

  • Cardiac rehab is offered free on the NHS to eligible patients
  • Ask your cardiologist or GP for a referral if you have not been offered it
  • Bring comfortable exercise clothing and appropriate footwear
  • The programme is tailored to your individual capacity - you will not be pushed beyond safe limits
author: Clinical Information Team

title: Preparing for Hip or Knee Replacement
excerpt: What to do in the weeks before your hip or knee replacement operation.
content:
A hip or knee replacement is a major operation and good preparation makes a significant difference to your recovery. These steps will help you get ready in the weeks and days before your surgery.

Prehabilitation

Prehabilitation means exercising and strengthening the muscles around your joint before surgery. Even a few weeks of targeted exercises significantly improves post-operative recovery. Your physiotherapy team may provide a prehabilitation programme. Swimming, cycling and gentle exercise in a pool are ideal if walking is painful.

Preparing Your Home

Before you come in for surgery, prepare your home for your return. Arrange for someone to be with you for at least the first week. Remove trip hazards (rugs, trailing cables). Consider a raised toilet seat, bed raisers and grab rails. Place frequently used items at waist height to avoid bending or reaching. Prepare and freeze meals in advance.

What to Bring to Hospital

Bring loose, comfortable clothing with elasticated waists. Bring well-fitting flat shoes (not slippers) as you will be walking with crutches or a frame. Bring your medication list and any splints or aids you currently use.

  • Stop smoking at least 6 weeks before surgery - it significantly improves healing
  • Aim to be as fit and strong as possible before the operation date
  • Lose weight if your surgeon has advised it - even small reductions improve outcomes
  • Arrange practical help at home for at least two weeks post-operatively
author: Clinical Information Team

title: Recovering from Hip Replacement
excerpt: A guide to what recovery from total hip replacement looks like week by week.
content:
Most people make an excellent recovery from hip replacement surgery and are walking with aids by the next day. Here is what to expect week by week during the recovery process.

Days 1 to 3: Early Mobility

Your physiotherapist will help you stand and walk with a frame or crutches the day after surgery. This early mobility is essential for preventing blood clots and beginning your recovery. You will be shown hip precautions - specific movements to avoid to prevent dislocation. These are critically important to follow.

Weeks 1 to 6: Gradual Recovery

Pain and swelling are normal and will improve week by week. Continue your home exercises daily. Use your walking aids as instructed and gradually reduce them as strength returns. Most people are walking without aids after 4 to 6 weeks. Avoid driving until your surgeon confirms it is safe, usually at the 6-week review.

Weeks 6 to 12 and Beyond

Most people can return to light work at 6 weeks, more physical jobs at 12 weeks or later. Swimming and cycling can usually be resumed at 6 weeks. Sports with impact or twisting (tennis, running) usually require 3 to 6 months of recovery. Your full recovery may take up to 12 months.

  • Do all prescribed exercises daily - consistency determines your outcome
  • Attend all physiotherapy appointments
  • Follow hip precautions exactly as instructed
  • Report any sudden increase in pain, fever, or wound changes to your surgical team immediately
author: Clinical Information Team

title: Fracture Care and Recovery
excerpt: What to expect after a fracture including cast care and the stages of bone healing.
content:
A fracture (broken bone) can be a painful and worrying experience. Understanding the healing process and how to care for your fracture properly helps your bone heal correctly and reduces the risk of complications.

Types of Fracture Treatment

Not all fractures require surgery. Many fractures are treated conservatively with a plaster cast, fibreglass cast or splint to hold the bone in the correct position while it heals. More complex or displaced fractures may require surgery to fix the bone with plates, screws, rods or pins.

Cast Care

Keep your cast clean and dry. Never poke objects inside to scratch - you may damage the skin and cause infection. Do not rest the cast on hard surfaces for prolonged periods as this can distort its shape. Elevate the limb to reduce swelling. Contact the fracture clinic if your cast feels too tight, if your fingers or toes become discoloured, very cold or numb, or if a new pain develops inside the cast.

The Stages of Bone Healing

Bone healing occurs in overlapping phases: inflammatory phase (days 1-5, pain and swelling), repair phase (weeks 2-6, soft callus forms), remodelling phase (months 2-12, hard bone replaces the callus). The total healing time depends on the bone, patient age, nutritional status and whether the fracture was surgically fixed.

  • Eat a calcium-rich diet and ensure adequate Vitamin D to support bone healing
  • Do not smoke - smoking significantly delays bone healing
  • Follow all weight-bearing instructions precisely
  • Attend all fracture clinic appointments as scheduled
author: Clinical Information Team

title: Your Guide to Physiotherapy
excerpt: What physiotherapy is what to expect from sessions and how to get the most from your treatment.
content:
Physiotherapy uses movement, exercise and physical techniques to help people recover from injury, surgery or illness, manage long-term conditions and prevent future problems. Here is what to expect and how to get the most from your physiotherapy care.

Your First Appointment

Your physiotherapist will take a detailed history of your condition, carry out a physical assessment and discuss your goals and expectations. They will explain their findings, give you a diagnosis if appropriate, and agree a treatment plan with you. This first appointment is usually the longest.

What Treatment Involves

Physiotherapy treatment is highly individualised. It may include therapeutic exercises (the most important component), manual therapy (joint mobilisation, manipulation, soft tissue work), electrotherapy, acupuncture and education about your condition and how to manage it. Home exercises are almost always central to the programme.

Getting the Most from Your Treatment

Physiotherapy outcomes are directly related to patient engagement. Doing your home exercises consistently, attending all appointments, communicating openly about what is and is not working, and setting clear realistic goals all significantly improve results. Be patient - most musculoskeletal conditions improve gradually over weeks to months.

  • Do your home exercises every day - this is where most of the benefit comes from
  • Tell your physiotherapist if your symptoms change significantly between sessions
  • Ask about what you can and cannot do between sessions
  • A good physiotherapist will aim to make themselves unnecessary over time
author: Clinical Information Team

title: Your Antenatal Care Guide
excerpt: A guide to the antenatal care appointments and tests you can expect during your pregnancy.
content:
Antenatal care refers to the healthcare and support you receive throughout your pregnancy. The NHS offers a structured programme of appointments, scans and tests designed to monitor your health and your baby's wellbeing from early pregnancy through to birth.

Your Booking Appointment

Your first midwife appointment (the booking appointment) usually takes place between 8 and 10 weeks of pregnancy. It is the longest appointment and covers your full medical history, lifestyle and social circumstances. Blood tests are taken to check blood group, anaemia, infection status and chromosomal conditions. You will be given information about vitamins, lifestyle and the appointments ahead.

Scans

You will be offered a 12-week scan (nuchal translucency scan) which checks the baby's development and screens for Down syndrome. The 20-week anomaly scan checks the baby's anatomy and growth in detail. Some women are offered additional scans for growth or wellbeing monitoring if there are risk factors.

Ongoing Appointments

You will be offered appointments with your midwife approximately every four weeks in early pregnancy, increasing in frequency in the third trimester. Each appointment checks your blood pressure, urine, fundal height (bump measurement) and baby's heartbeat, and addresses any questions or concerns you have.

  • Take folic acid 400 mcg daily until 12 weeks
  • Take Vitamin D 10 mcg daily throughout pregnancy
  • Attend all appointments - they are designed to detect problems early
  • Write down questions between appointments so you do not forget them
author: Clinical Information Team

title: What to Expect During Labour and Birth
excerpt: A guide to the stages of labour and what to expect when you come in to have your baby.
content:
Labour and birth can feel daunting, particularly for first-time parents. Understanding what to expect at each stage reduces anxiety and helps you feel more in control. Birth is unpredictable, but being prepared makes a significant difference.

Recognising the Start of Labour

Labour begins with regular, rhythmical contractions that increase in frequency, duration and intensity over time. You may also experience a show (a pink or blood-tinged mucus plug) or your waters breaking. If your contractions are more than five minutes apart in early labour, most women can stay at home and rest. Contact your maternity unit if you are unsure, in pain, if your waters break or if there is any bleeding.

The Three Stages of Labour

The first stage involves the cervix dilating from 0 to 10 cm. This is the longest part of labour, particularly for first-time mothers. The second stage begins when the cervix is fully dilated and ends with the birth of the baby. The third stage is the delivery of the placenta, which usually takes 15 to 30 minutes.

Pain Relief Options

You can choose from a range of pain relief options including gas and air (Entonox), TENS machine (most effective in early labour), opioid injections (diamorphine or pethidine), and epidural (the most effective option for complete pain relief). You do not need to commit to a plan in advance - your needs may change during labour.

  • Write a birth plan but remain flexible - labour is unpredictable
  • Bring a birth partner for continuous support throughout
  • Pack your hospital bag by 36 weeks
  • Trust your midwife team - they are experts in supporting normal birth
author: Clinical Information Team

title: Making the Most of Your Outpatient Appointment
excerpt: How to prepare for your outpatient consultation and get the most from your time with the consultant.
content:
An outpatient appointment with a specialist consultant is a valuable opportunity to discuss your symptoms, understand your diagnosis and agree a management plan. Making the most of your time ensures you leave with the information and next steps you need.

Preparing for Your Appointment

Write down your main symptoms including when they started, what makes them better or worse, and how they affect your daily life. Bring a complete list of all medications and their dosages. Write down your questions in order of priority so the most important ones get answered even if time is short.

During Your Appointment

Be as specific as possible when describing your symptoms. If you do not understand something, ask the consultant to explain it differently. It is entirely reasonable to ask why a test or treatment is being recommended. If you would like a friend or family member to accompany you for support or to help remember information, this is usually welcomed.

After Your Appointment

A clinic letter summarising the appointment will usually be sent to your GP. Keep a copy for your records. If you are given new medication, ensure you understand what it is for and any side effects to watch for. If you have further questions after the appointment, contact the consultant's secretary.

  • Write down key information during the appointment or ask if you can record it on your phone
  • Make sure you know what happens next: investigations, further appointments, referrals
  • Ask about the timeframe for results and how you will be informed
  • Do not be afraid to ask questions - there is no such thing as a stupid question
author: Clinical Information Team

title: Understanding Common Tests and Investigations
excerpt: What to expect from blood tests X-rays MRI scans and other common investigations.
content:
Being sent for tests or investigations can cause anxiety, particularly if you do not know what they involve. This guide explains what the most common tests involve, how to prepare and how results are communicated.

Blood Tests

Blood tests are the most common type of investigation. A healthcare professional takes a small sample of blood from a vein in your arm. Results are usually available within a few days. Your GP or consultant will interpret your results in the context of your symptoms and health history rather than simply comparing to reference ranges.

Imaging: X-ray, Ultrasound and CT

X-rays use radiation to produce images of bones and some soft tissues. They are quick and painless. Ultrasound uses sound waves (no radiation) to produce images of soft tissue structures. CT (computed tomography) scans produce detailed cross-sectional images using X-rays; they are more detailed than plain X-rays but deliver more radiation. You will be asked about allergies and medications before CT scans requiring contrast dye.

MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging)

MRI uses a powerful magnetic field and radio waves (no radiation) to produce highly detailed images of soft tissues, joints and organs. The scan involves lying still inside a large tube for 20-60 minutes. It can be noisy. Tell the department beforehand if you have any metal implants, pacemakers or claustrophobia.

  • Follow all preparation instructions for your specific test
  • Bring a list of medications to any imaging appointment requiring contrast
  • Ask how and when your results will be communicated to you
  • Ask what the next step will be depending on the results
author: Clinical Information Team
